column4: Ensure that the API server pod specification file permissions are set to 600 or more restrictive
column5: Ensure that the API server pod specification file has permissions of 600 or more restrictive.
column6: The API server pod specification file controls various parameters that set the behavior of the API server. You should restrict its file permissions to maintain the integrity of the file. The file should be writable by only the administrators on the system. Impact: None
column7: Run the below command (based on the file location on your system) on the Control Plane node. For example, stat -c %a /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml Verify that the permissions are 600 or more restrictive.
column8: Run the below command (based on the file location on your system) on the Control Plane node. For example, chmod 600 /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml Default Value: By default, the kube-apiserver.yaml file has permissions of 640. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kube-apiserver/
column19: The task involves checking file permissions on the host operating system, which cannot be directly performed using Kubernetes APIs or `kubectl` as it involves the file system of the underlying node. Both AI opinions agree that this is a manual task, and while automation through configuration management tools like Ansible or Puppet is possible, it is outside the scope of Kubernetes-native automation.
column21: 1. SSH into the Control Plane node where the API server pod specification file is located. 2. Run the command: `stat -c %a /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml`. 3. Verify that the output shows the permissions set to `600` or more restrictive (e.g., `400`). 4. If the permissions are not set to `600` or more restrictive, it is a compliance failure. Remediate by running `chmod 600 /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml` to set the appropriate permissions.

Those files should be writable by only the administrators on the system. Impact: None

column8: Run the below command (based on the file location on your system) on the Control Plane node. For example, chmod 600 <path/to/cni/files> Default Value: NA References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/cluster-administration/networking/
column19: The task involves checking and setting file permissions on the host system where the Container Network Interface (CNI) files reside. This is outside the scope of Kubernetes APIs and CLI capabilities, which manage Kubernetes resources rather than host file system permissions. Both AI opinions acknowledge the necessity of manual intervention or external automation tools, but since Kubernetes itself does not provide direct methods for this, a manual approach is required.
column21: 1. SSH into the Control Plane node where the Container Network Interface (CNI) files are located. 2. Navigate to the directory where the CNI files are stored, typically `/etc/cni/net.d/` or a similar path depending on your configuration. 3. Run the command `stat -c %a <path/to/cni/files>` for each CNI file to check the file permissions. 4. Verify that the permissions are set to `600` or more restrictive (e.g., `400`). 5. If any file permissions are found to be less restrictive than `600`, run the command `chmod 600 <path/to/cni/files>` to correct the permissions. 6. Re-run the `stat -c %a <path/to/cni/files>` command to ensure that the permissions are now set to `600` or more restrictive.

column4: Ensure that the etcd data directory permissions are set to 700 or more restrictive
column5: Ensure that the etcd data directory has permissions of 700 or more restrictive.
column6: etcd is a highly-available key-value store used by Kubernetes deployments for persistent storage of all of its REST API objects. This data directory should be protected from any unauthorized reads or writes. It should not be readable or writable by any group members or the world. Impact: None
column7: On the etcd server node, get the etcd data directory, passed as an argument --data- dir, from the below command: ps -ef | grep etcd Run the below command (based on the etcd data directory found above). For example, stat -c %a /var/lib/etcd Verify that the permissions are 700 or more restrictive.
column8: On the etcd server node, get the etcd data directory, passed as an argument --data- dir, from the below command: ps -ef | grep etcd Run the below command (based on the etcd data directory found above). For example, chmod 700 /var/lib/etcd Default Value: By default, etcd data directory has permissions of 755. References: 1. https://coreos.com/etcd/docs/latest/op-guide/configuration.html#data-dir 2. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/etcd/
column19: The task involves checking and setting file permissions on the host operating system where etcd runs, which is outside the scope of Kubernetes' native capabilities. This requires direct access to the server's file system, typically via SSH, as Kubernetes APIs do not provide functionality to manage host-level file permissions.
column21: 1. SSH into the etcd server node. 2. Execute `ps -ef | grep etcd` to identify the etcd process and locate the `--data-dir` argument, which specifies the etcd data directory. 3. Use `stat -c %a <path_to_etcd_data_directory>` (replace `<path_to_etcd_data_directory>` with the actual path found in the previous step) to check the permissions of the etcd data directory. 4. Verify that the permissions are set to 700 or more restrictive (e.g., 600). 5. If the permissions are not compliant, run `chmod 700 <path_to_etcd_data_directory>` to remediate the issue.

column7: On the etcd server node, get the etcd data directory, passed as an argument --data- dir, from the below command: ps -ef | grep etcd Run the below command (based on the etcd data directory found above). For example, stat -c %U:%G /var/lib/etcd Verify that the ownership is set to etcd:etcd.

column4: Ensure that the default administrative credential file permissions are set to 600
column5: Ensure that the admin.conf file (and super-admin.conf file, where it exists) have permissions of 600.
column6: As part of initial cluster setup, default kubeconfig files are created to be used by the administrator of the cluster. These files contain private keys and certificates which allow for privileged access to the cluster. You should restrict their file permissions to maintain the integrity and confidentiality of the file(s). The file(s) should be readable and writable by only the administrators on the system. Impact: None.
column7: Run the following command (based on the file location on your system) on the Control Plane node. For example, stat -c %a /etc/kubernetes/admin.conf On Kubernetes version 1.29 and higher run the following command as well :- stat -c %a /etc/kubernetes/super-admin.conf Verify that the permissions are 600 or more restrictive.
column8: Run the below command (based on the file location on your system) on the Control Plane node. For example, chmod 600 /etc/kubernetes/admin.conf On Kubernetes 1.29+ the super-admin.conf file should also be modified, if present. For example, chmod 600 /etc/kubernetes/super-admin.conf Default Value: By default, admin.conf and super-admin.conf have permissions of 600. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/setup/independent/create-cluster-kubeadm/ 2. https://raesene.github.io/blog/2024/01/06/when-is-admin-not-admin/
column19: The compliance control involves checking and setting file permissions on the host system, which is outside the scope of Kubernetes APIs or `kubectl` capabilities. Confirm that the permissions have been updated correctly by re-running the `stat` commands and ensuring they now reflect 600.

column4: Client certificate authentication should not be used for users
column5: Kubernetes provides the option to use client certificates for user authentication. However as there is no way to revoke these certificates when a user leaves an organization or loses their credential, they are not suitable for this purpose. It is not possible to fully disable client certificate use within a cluster as it is used for component to component authentication.
column6: With any authentication mechanism the ability to revoke credentials if they are compromised or no longer required, is a key control. column29: 1. Identify the kubelet configuration file path by checking the kubelet's command line arguments on each worker node. Verify that the file permissions are set to `600` or more restrictive (e.g., `400`). 4. If the permissions are not compliant, use the command `chmod 600 /var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml` to correct them.

column4: Ensure that the --authorization-mode argument is not set to AlwaysAllow
column5: Do not allow all requests. Enable explicit authorization.
column6: Kubelets, by default, allow all authenticated requests (even anonymous ones) without needing explicit authorization checks from the apiserver. You should restrict this behavior and only allow explicitly authorized requests. Impact: Unauthorized requests will be denied.
column7: Run the following command on each node: ps -ef | grep kubelet If the --authorization-mode argument is present check that it is not set to AlwaysAllow. If it is not present check that there is a Kubelet config file specified by -- config, and that file sets authorization: mode to something other than AlwaysAllow. It is also possible to review the running configuration of a Kubelet via the /configz endpoint on the Kubelet API port (typically 10250/TCP). Accessing these with appropriate credentials will provide details of the Kubelet's configuration.
column8: If using a Kubelet config file, edit the file to set authorization: mode to Webhook. If using executable arguments, edit the kubelet service file /etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf on each worker node and set the below parameter in KUBELET_AUTHZ_ARGS variable. --authorization-mode=Webhook Based on your system, restart the kubelet service. For example: systemctl daemon-reload systemctl restart kubelet.service Default Value: By default, --authorization-mode argument is set to AlwaysAllow. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kubelet/ 2. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kubelet-authentication-authorization/#kubelet- authentication

column4: Ensure that the --client-ca-file argument is set as appropriate
column5: Enable Kubelet authentication using certificates.
column6: The connections from the apiserver to the kubelet are used for fetching logs for pods, attaching (through kubectl) to running pods, and using the kubelet’s port-forwarding functionality. These connections terminate at the kubelet’s HTTPS endpoint. By default, the apiserver does not verify the kubelet’s serving certificate, which makes the connection subject to man-in-the-middle attacks, and unsafe to run over untrusted and/or public networks. Enabling Kubelet certificate authentication ensures that the apiserver could authenticate the Kubelet before submitting any requests. Impact: You require TLS to be configured on apiserver as well as kubelets.
column7: Run the following command on each node: ps -ef | grep kubelet Verify that the --client-ca-file argument exists and is set to the location of the client certificate authority file. If the --client-ca-file argument is not present, check that there is a Kubelet config file specified by --config, and that the file sets authentication: x509: clientCAFile to the location of the client certificate authority file.
column8: If using a Kubelet config file, edit the file to set authentication: x509: clientCAFile to the location of the client CA file. If using command line arguments, edit the kubelet service file /etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf on each worker node and set the below parameter in KUBELET_AUTHZ_ARGS variable. --client-ca-file=<path/to/client-ca-file> Based on your system, restart the kubelet service. For example: systemctl daemon-reload systemctl restart kubelet.service Default Value: By default, --client-ca-file argument is not set. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kubelet/ 2. https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/command-line-tools-reference/kubelet- authentication-authorization/

column4: Ensure that the --make-iptables-util-chains argument is set to true
column5: Allow Kubelet to manage iptables.
column6: Kubelets can automatically manage the required changes to iptables based on how you choose your networking options for the pods. It is recommended to let kubelets manage the changes to iptables. This ensures that the iptables configuration remains in sync with pods networking configuration. Manually configuring iptables with dynamic pod network configuration changes might hamper the communication between pods/containers and to the outside world. You might have iptables rules too restrictive or too open. Impact: Kubelet would manage the iptables on the system and keep it in sync. If you are using any other iptables management solution, then there might be some conflicts.
column7: Run the following command on each node: ps -ef | grep kubelet Verify that if the --make-iptables-util-chains argument exists then it is set to true. If the --make-iptables-util-chains argument does not exist, and there is a Kubelet config file specified by --config, verify that the file does not set makeIPTablesUtilChains to false.
column8: If using a Kubelet config file, edit the file to set makeIPTablesUtilChains: true. If using command line arguments, edit the kubelet service file /etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf on each worker node and remove the --make- iptables-util-chains argument from the KUBELET_SYSTEM_PODS_ARGS variable. Based on your system, restart the kubelet service. For example: systemctl daemon-reload systemctl restart kubelet.service Default Value: By default, --make-iptables-util-chains argument is set to true. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kubelet/
column10: Kubelet would manage the iptables on the system and keep it in sync. If you are using any other iptables management solution, then there might be some conflicts.

column4: Ensure that the --hostname-override argument is not set
column5: Do not override node hostnames.
column6: Overriding hostnames could potentially break TLS setup between the kubelet and the apiserver. Additionally, with overridden hostnames, it becomes increasingly difficult to associate logs with a particular node and process them for security analytics. Hence, you should setup your kubelet nodes with resolvable FQDNs and avoid overriding the hostnames with IPs. Impact: Some cloud providers may require this flag to ensure that hostname matches names issued by the cloud provider. In these environments, this recommendation should not apply.
column7: Run the following command on each node: ps -ef | grep kubelet Verify that --hostname-override argument does not exist. Note This setting is not configurable via the Kubelet config file.
column8: Edit the kubelet service file /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10- kubeadm.conf on each worker node and remove the --hostname-override argument from the KUBELET_SYSTEM_PODS_ARGS variable. Based on your system, restart the kubelet service. For example: systemctl daemon-reload systemctl restart kubelet.service Default Value: By default, --hostname-override argument is not set. column4: Ensure that the eventRecordQPS argument is set to a level which ensures appropriate event capture
column5: Security relevant information should be captured. The eventRecordQPS on the Kubelet configuration can be used to limit the rate at which events are gathered and sets the maximum event creations per second. Setting this too low could result in relevant events not being logged, however the unlimited setting of 0 could result in a denial of service on the kubelet.
column6: It is important to capture all events and not restrict event creation. Events are an important source of security information and analytics that ensure that your environment is consistently monitored using the event data. Impact: Setting this parameter to 0 could result in a denial of service condition due to excessive events being created. The cluster's event processing and storage systems should be scaled to handle expected event loads.
column7: Run the following command on each node: sudo grep "eventRecordQPS" /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10- kubeadm.conf or If using command line arguments, kubelet service file is located /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubelet-args.conf sudo grep "eventRecordQPS" /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubelet- args.conf Review the value set for the argument and determine whether this has been set to an appropriate level for the cluster. If the argument does not exist, check that there is a Kubelet config file specified by -- config and review the value in this location. If using command line arguments
column8: If using a Kubelet config file, edit the file to set eventRecordQPS: to an appropriate level. Based on your system, restart the kubelet service. For example: systemctl daemon-reload systemctl restart kubelet.service Default Value: By default, eventRecordQPS argument is set to 5. column4: Verify that the RotateKubeletServerCertificate argument is set to true
column5: Enable kubelet server certificate rotation.
column6: RotateKubeletServerCertificate causes the kubelet to both request a serving certificate after bootstrapping its client credentials and rotate the certificate as its existing credentials expire. This automated periodic rotation ensures that the there are no downtimes due to expired certificates and thus addressing availability in the CIA security triad. Note: This recommendation only applies if you let kubelets get their certificates from the API server. In case your kubelet certificates come from an outside authority/tool (e.g. Vault) then you need to take care of rotation yourself. Impact: None
column7: Ignore this check if serverTLSBootstrap is true in the kubelet config file or if the --rotate- server-certificates parameter is set on kubelet Run the following command on each node: ps -ef | grep kubelet Verify that RotateKubeletServerCertificate argument exists and is set to true.
column8: Edit the kubelet service file /etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf on each worker node and set the below parameter in KUBELET_CERTIFICATE_ARGS variable. --feature-gates=RotateKubeletServerCertificate=true Based on your system, restart the kubelet service. For example: systemctl daemon-reload systemctl restart kubelet.service Default Value: By default, kubelet server certificate rotation is enabled. References: 1. https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/45059 2. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kubelet-tls-bootstrapping/#kubelet-configuration

column4: Ensure that the Kubelet only makes use of Strong Cryptographic Ciphers
column5: Ensure that the Kubelet is configured to only use strong cryptographic ciphers.
column6: TLS ciphers have had a number of known vulnerabilities and weaknesses, which can reduce the protection provided by them. By default Kubernetes supports a number of TLS ciphersuites including some that have security concerns, weakening the protection provided. Impact: Kubelet clients that cannot support modern cryptographic ciphers will not be able to make connections to the Kubelet API.
column7: The set of cryptographic ciphers currently considered secure is the following: • TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256 • TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256 • TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_CHACHA20_POLY1305 • TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384 • TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_CHACHA20_POLY1305 • TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384 • TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384 • TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256 Run the following command on each node: ps -ef | grep kubelet If the --tls-cipher-suites argument is present, ensure it only contains values included in this set. If it is not present check that there is a Kubelet config file specified by --config, and that file sets tlsCipherSuites: to only include values from this set.
column8: If using a Kubelet config file, edit the file to set tlsCipherSuites: to TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256,TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM _SHA256,TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_CHACHA20_POLY1305,TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_ 256_GCM_SHA384,TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_CHACHA20_POLY1305,TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WI TH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384,TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384,TLS_RSA_WITH_AES _128_GCM_SHA256 or to a subset of these values. If using executable arguments, edit the kubelet service file /etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf on each worker node and set the --tls-cipher- suites parameter as follows, or to a subset of these values. --tls-cipher- suites=TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256,TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM _SHA256,TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_CHACHA20_POLY1305,TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM _SHA384,TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_CHACHA20_POLY1305,TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM _SHA384,TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384,TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256 Based on your system, restart the kubelet service. For example: systemctl daemon-reload systemctl restart kubelet.service Default Value: By default the Kubernetes API server supports a wide range of TLS ciphers Additional Information: The list chosen above should be fine for modern clients. It's essentially the list from the Mozilla "Modern cipher" option with the ciphersuites supporting CBC mode removed, as CBC has traditionally had a lot of issues

column4: Ensure that the --IPAddressDeny is set to any
column5: Ensuring that --IPAddressDeny is set to "Any" will facilitate allowlisting of only IP addresses that are explicitly set with the --IPAddressAllow parameter which will block unspecified IP addresses from communicating with the kubelet component.
column6: By default, Kubernetes allows any IP address to communicate with the kubelet component IP restrictions and IP whitelisting are security best practices and reduce the attack surface of the kubelet . Impact: Configuring the setting IPAddressDeny=any will deny service to any IP address not specified in the complimentary setting IPAddressDeny=any configuration parameter. Applying IPAddressDeny=any alone will completely disable communication with the component.
column7: Review the Kubelet's start-up parameters for the value of --IPAddressDeny, and check the Kubelet configuration file for IPAddressDeny=any. If this entry is present it should be accompanied by IPAddressAllow={{ kubelet_secure_addresses }} to allow the control plane to communicate with the component.
column8: IPAddressDeny=any IPAddressAllow={{ kubelet_secure_addresses }} *Note kubelet_secure_addresses: "localhost link-local {{ kube_pods_subnets | regex_replace(',', ' ') }} {{ kube_node_addresses }} {{ loadbalancer_apiserver.address | default('')" Default Value: By default IPAddressDeny is not enabled. References: 1. https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/kubespray/pull/9194/files 2. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/services-networking/network-policies/
column10: Configuring the setting IPAddressDeny=any will deny service to any IP address not specified in the complimentary setting IPAddressDeny=any configuration parameter. Applying IPAddressDeny=any alone will completely disable communication with the component.

column4: Ensure that the CNI in use supports Network Policies
column5: There are a variety of CNI plugins available for Kubernetes. If the CNI in use does not support Network Policies it may not be possible to effectively restrict traffic in the cluster.
column6: Kubernetes network policies are enforced by the CNI plugin in use. As such it is important to ensure that the CNI plugin supports both Ingress and Egress network policies. Impact: None
column7: Review the documentation of CNI plugin in use by the cluster, and confirm that it supports Ingress and Egress network policies.
column8: If the CNI plugin in use does not support network policies, consideration should be given to making use of a different plugin, or finding an alternate mechanism for restricting traffic in the Kubernetes cluster. Default Value: This will depend on the CNI plugin in use. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/extend-kubernetes/compute-storage- net/network-plugins/ Additional Information: One example here is Flannel (https://github.com/coreos/flannel) which does not support Network policy unless Calico is also in use.

column19: While the identification of the CNI plugin in use can be automated using Kubernetes APIs or `kubectl`, verifying whether the CNI plugin supports Network Policies requires consulting external documentation, which is inherently a manual process. The support for Ingress and Egress policies is not directly exposed via Kubernetes APIs or configurations, necessitating a review of the CNI plugin's documentation.
column21: 1. Use `kubectl get pods -n kube-system -o wide` to identify the CNI plugin in use by examining the pods running in the `kube-system` namespace. 2. Locate the CNI configuration files on the nodes, typically found in `/etc/cni/net.d/`, to confirm the CNI plugin in use. 3. Refer to the official documentation of the identified CNI plugin to verify its support for both Ingress and Egress network policies. 4. Ensure that the documentation explicitly states support for these policies to confirm compliance.

column4: Consider external secret storage
column5: Consider the use of an external secrets storage and management system, instead of using Kubernetes Secrets directly, if you have more complex secret management needs. Ensure the solution requires authentication to access secrets, has auditing of access to and use of secrets, and encrypts secrets. Some solutions also make it easier to rotate secrets.
column6: Kubernetes supports secrets as first-class objects, but care needs to be taken to ensure that access to secrets is carefully limited. Using an external secrets provider can ease the management of access to secrets, especially where secrests are used across both Kubernetes and non-Kubernetes environments. Impact: None
column7: Review your secrets management implementation.
column8: Refer to the secrets management options offered by your cloud provider or a third-party secrets management solution. Default Value: By default, no external secret management is configured.
column19: While the presence of external secret management solutions can be detected using Kubernetes APIs and kubectl commands, verifying the specific criteria of authentication, auditing, and encryption requires manual review of the external solution's documentation or configuration. These security features are not directly verifiable through Kubernetes APIs alone, necessitating a manual approach to ensure compliance with the control's requirements.
column21: 1. **Identify External Secret Management Solutions:** - Use the command `kubectl get crds` to list all Custom Resource Definitions and identify any related to external secrets, such as `ExternalSecret`. - Use `kubectl get externalsecrets --all-namespaces` to list instances of these resources. 2. **Review Solution Documentation:** - Access the documentation of the identified external secret management solution. - Verify that the solution requires authentication to access secrets, supports auditing of access and use, and encrypts secrets. 3. **Configuration Verification:** - Check the configuration files or settings of the external secret management solution to ensure the security features (authentication, auditing, encryption) are enabled and correctly configured. 4. **Confirm Compliance:** - Ensure that the external secret management solution is actively used in the cluster and meets all security requirements.

column4: Ensure that the --authorization-mode argument is not set to AlwaysAllow
column5: Do not allow all requests. Enable explicit authorization.
column6: Kubelets, by default, allow all authenticated requests (even anonymous ones) without needing explicit authorization checks from the apiserver. You should restrict this behavior and only allow explicitly authorized requests.
column7: Run the following command on each node: ps -ef | grep kubelet If the --authorization-mode argument is present check that it is not set to AlwaysAllow . If it is not present check that there is a Kubelet config file specified by -- config , and that file sets authorization: mode to something other than AlwaysAllow . It is also possible to review the running configuration of a Kubelet via the /configz endpoint on the Kubelet API port (typically 10250/TCP ). Accessing these with appropriate credentials will provide details of the Kubelet's configuration.
column8: If using a Kubelet config file, edit the file to set authorization: mode to Webhook . If using executable arguments, edit the kubelet service file /etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf on each worker node and set the below parameter in KUBELET_AUTHZ_ARGS variable. --authorization-mode=Webhook Based on your system, restart the kubelet service. For example: Internal Only - General systemctl daemon-reload systemctl restart kubelet.service
column14: By default, --authorization-mode argument is set to AlwaysAllow .
column19: The task requires checking the Kubelet's `--authorization-mode` setting, which is typically configured via command-line arguments or a configuration file on each node. Therefore, manual inspection of the node's configuration files or command-line arguments is necessary.
column21: 1. SSH into each Kubernetes node where the Kubelet is running. 2. Run the command `ps -ef | grep kubelet` to inspect the Kubelet's command-line arguments. 3. If changes are made, restart the Kubelet service using `systemctl daemon-reload` and `systemctl restart kubelet.service`.

column4: Ensure that the --client-ca-file argument is set as appropriate
column5: Enable Kubelet authentication using certificates.
column6: The connections from the apiserver to the kubelet are used for fetching logs for pods, attaching (through kubectl) to running pods, and using the kubelet’s port-forwarding functionality. These connections terminate at the kubelet’s HTTPS endpoint. By default, the apiserver does not verify the kubelet’s serving certificate, which makes the connection subject to man-in-the-middle attacks, and unsafe to run over untrusted and/or public networks. Enabling Kubelet certificate authentication ensures that the apiserver could authenticate the Kubelet before submitting any requests.
column7: Run the following command on each node: ps -ef | grep kubelet Verify that the --client-ca-file argument exists and is set to the location of the client certificate authority file. If the --client-ca-file argument is not present, check that there is a Kubelet config file specified by --config , and that the file sets authentication: x509: clientCAFile to the location of the client certificate authority file.
column8: If using a Kubelet config file, edit the file to set authentication: x509: clientCAFile to the location of the client CA file. If using command line arguments, edit the kubelet service file /etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf on each worker node and set the below parameter in KUBELET_AUTHZ_ARGS variable. --client-ca-file=<path/to/client-ca-file> Based on your system, restart the kubelet service. For example: Internal Only - General systemctl daemon-reload systemctl restart kubelet.service

Manually configuring iptables with dynamic pod network configuration changes might hamper the communication between pods/containers and to the outside world. You might have iptables rules too restrictive or too open.

column4: Ensure that the --rotate-certificates argument is not set to false
column5: Enable kubelet client certificate rotation.
column6: The --rotate-certificates setting causes the kubelet to rotate its client certificates by creating new CSRs as its existing credentials expire. This automated periodic rotation ensures that the there is no downtime due to expired certificates and thus addressing availability in the CIA security triad. Note: This recommendation only applies if you let kubelets get their certificates from the API server. In case your kubelet certificates come from an outside authority/tool (e.g. Vault) then you need to take care of rotation yourself. Note: This feature also require the RotateKubeletClientCertificate feature gate to be enabled (which is the default since Kubernetes v1.7)
column7: Run the following command on each node: ps -ef | grep kubelet Verify that the RotateKubeletServerCertificate argument is not present, or is set to true . If the RotateKubeletServerCertificate argument is not present, verify that if there is a Kubelet config file specified by --config , that file does not contain RotateKubeletServerCertificate: false .
column8: If using a Kubelet config file, edit the file to add the line rotateCertificates: true or remove it altogether to use the default value. If using command line arguments, edit the kubelet service file /etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf on each worker node and remove --rotate- certificates=false argument from the KUBELET_CERTIFICATE_ARGS variable or set - -rotate-certificates=true . Internal Only - General Based on your system, restart the kubelet service. For example: systemctl daemon-reload systemctl restart kubelet.service

column4: Ensure that the CNI in use supports Network Policies
column5: There are a variety of CNI plugins available for Kubernetes. If the CNI in use does not support Network Policies it may not be possible to effectively restrict traffic in the cluster.
column6: Kubernetes network policies are enforced by the CNI plugin in use. As such it is important to ensure that the CNI plugin supports both Ingress and Egress network policies.
column7: Review the documentation of CNI plugin in use by the cluster, and confirm that it supports Ingress and Egress network policies.
column8: If the CNI plugin in use does not support network policies, consideration should be given to making use of a different plugin, or finding an alternate mechanism for restricting traffic in the Kubernetes cluster.
column14: This will depend on the CNI plugin in use.
column15: One example here is Flannel ( https://github.com/coreos/flannel ) which does not support Network policy unless Calico is also in use. Internal Only - General
column19: While identifying the CNI plugin in use can be automated using Kubernetes APIs, confirming whether it supports Network Policies requires consulting external documentation or configuration details that are not directly accessible through Kubernetes APIs. This makes it necessary to manually verify the CNI plugin's capabilities against its documentation.
column21: 1. Use `kubectl get pods -n kube-system` to identify the CNI plugin in use by listing the pods in the `kube-system` namespace. 2. Refer to the official documentation of the identified CNI plugin to verify if it supports both Ingress and Egress Network Policies. 3. Ensure that the documentation explicitly states support for Network Policies, as this is crucial for compliance. 4. If the CNI plugin does not support Network Policies, consider switching to a CNI that does or implement an alternative mechanism for traffic restriction.

column4: Consider external secret storage
column5: Consider the use of an external secrets storage and management system, instead of using Kubernetes Secrets directly, if you have more complex secret management needs. Ensure the solution requires authentication to access secrets, has auditing of access to and use of secrets, and encrypts secrets. Some solutions also make it easier to rotate secrets.
column6: Kubernetes supports secrets as first-class objects, but care needs to be taken to ensure that access to secrets is carefully limited. Using an external secrets provider can ease the management of access to secrets, especially where secrests are used across both Kubernetes and non-Kubernetes environments.
column7: Review your secrets management implementation.
column8: Refer to the secrets management options offered by your cloud provider or a third-party secrets management solution.
column14: By default, no external secret management is configured.
column19: While some aspects of checking Kubernetes Secrets usage can be automated using Kubernetes CLI commands like `kubectl get secrets`, the comprehensive evaluation of external secret management solutions involves verifying authentication, auditing, encryption, and secret rotation capabilities. These aspects typically require manual review of external systems and their configurations, which are not directly accessible or verifiable through Kubernetes APIs.
column21: 1. Review your current secrets management implementation: - Determine if Kubernetes Secrets are being used directly or if an external secrets management solution is in place. 2. Verify the authentication mechanism of the external secrets management solution: - Ensure that it requires authentication to access secrets. 3. Check for auditing capabilities: - Confirm that the solution provides auditing features to track access to and usage of secrets. 4. Assess encryption: - Verify that the secrets are encrypted both at rest and in transit. 5. Evaluate secret rotation: - Determine if the solution facilitates easier rotation of secrets. 6. Document the findings from the review and ensure that all criteria are met. Identify any gaps and plan for remediation if necessary.

column4: Ensure that a unique Certificate Authority is used for etcd
column5: Use a different certificate authority for etcd from the one used for Kubernetes.
column6: etcd is a highly available key-value store used by Kubernetes deployments for persistent storage of all of its REST API objects. Its access should be restricted to specifically designated clients and peers only. Authentication to etcd is based on whether the certificate presented was issued by a trusted certificate authority. There is no checking of certificate attributes such as common name or subject alternative name. As such, if any attackers were able to gain access to any certificate issued by the trusted certificate authority, they would be able to gain full access to the etcd database.
column7: Review the CA used by the etcd environment and ensure that it does not match the CA certificate file used for the management of the overall Kubernetes cluster. Run the following command on the master node: ps -ef | grep etcd Note the file referenced by the --trusted-ca-file argument. Run the following command on the master node: ps -ef | grep apiserver Verify that the file referenced by the --client-ca-file for apiserver is different from the - -trusted-ca-file used by etcd.
column8: 155 | P a g e Follow the etcd documentation and create a dedicated certificate authority setup for the etcd service. Then, edit the etcd pod specification file /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml on the master node and set the below parameter. --trusted-ca-file=</path/to/ca-file>

column4: Client certificate authentication should not be used for users
column5: Kubernetes provides the option to use client certificates for user authentication. However as there is no way to revoke these certificates when a user leaves an organization or loses their credential, they are not suitable for this purpose. It is not possible to fully disable client certificate use within a cluster as it is used for component to component authentication.
column6: With any authentication mechanism the ability to revoke credentials if they are compromised or no longer required, is a key control. Kubernetes client certificate authentication does not allow for this due to a lack of support for certificate revocation.
column7: Review user access to the cluster and ensure that users are not making use of Kubernetes client certificate authentication.
column8: Alternative mechanisms provided by Kubernetes such as the use of OIDC should be implemented in place of client certificates.
column10: External mechanisms for authentication generally require additional software to be deployed.

column6: The connections from the apiserver to the kubelet are used for fetching logs for pods, attaching (through kubectl) to running pods, and using the kubelet’s port -forwarding functionality. These connections terminate at the kubelet’s HTTPS endpoint. By default, the apiserver does not verify the kubelet’s serving certifi cate, which makes the connection subject to man-in-the-middle attacks, and unsafe to run over untrusted and/or public networks. Enabling Kubelet certificate authentication ensures that the apiserver could authenticate the Kubelet before submitting any requests.

Hence, you should setup your kubelet nodes with resolvable FQDNs and avoid overriding the hostnames with IPs.

column4: Ensure that the --tls-cert-file and --tls-private-key-file arguments are set as appropriate
column5: Setup TLS connection on the Kubelets.
column6: The connections from the apiserver to the kubelet are used for fetching logs for pods, attaching (through kubectl) to running pods, and using the kubelet’s port -forwarding functionality. These connections terminate at the kubelet’s HTTPS endpoint. By default, the apiserver does not verify the kubelet’s servin g certificate, which makes the connection subject to man-in-the-middle attacks, and unsafe to run over untrusted and/or public networks.
column7: Run the following command on each node: ps -ef | grep kubelet Verify that the --tls-cert-file and --tls-private-key-file arguments exist and they are set as appropriate. column8: If using a Kubelet config file, edit the file to set tlsCertFile to the location of the certificate file to use to identify this Kubelet, and tlsPrivateKeyFile to the location of the corresponding private key file. column4: Ensure that the --rotate-certificates argument is not set to false
column5: Enable kubelet client certificate rotation.
column6: The --rotate-certificates setting causes the kubelet to rotate its client certificates by creating new CSRs as its existing credentials expire. This automated periodic rotation ensures that the there is no downtime due to expired certificates and thus addressing availability in the CIA security triad. Note: This recommendation only applies if you let kubelets get their certificates from the API server. In case your kubelet certificates come from an outside authority/tool (e.g. Vault) then you need to take care of rotation yourself. Note: This feature also require the RotateKubeletClientCertificate feature gate to be enabled (which is the default since Kubernetes v1.7)
column7: Run the following command on each node: ps -ef | grep kubelet Verify that the --rotate-certificates argument is not present, or is set to true . If the --rotate-certificates argument is not present, verify that if there is a Kubelet config file specified by --config , that file does not contain rotateCertificates: false .
column8: If using a Kubelet config file, edit the file to add the line rotateCertificates: true or remove it altogether to use the default value. If using command line arguments, edit the kubelet service file 206 | P a g e /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubeadm.conf on each worker node and remove --rotate-certificates=false argument from the KUBELET_CERTIFICATE_ARGS variable. column4: Ensure that the Kubelet only makes use of Strong Cryptographic Ciphers
column5: Ensure that the Kubelet is configured to only use strong cryptographic ciphers.
column6: TLS ciphers have had a number of known vulnerabilities and weaknesses, which can reduce the protection provided by them. By default Kubernetes supports a number of TLS ciphersuites including some that have security concerns, weakening the protection provided.
column7: The set of cryptographic ciphers currently considered secure is the following: • TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256 • TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256 • TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_CHACHA20_POLY1305 • TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384 • TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_CHACHA20_POLY1305 • TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384 • TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384 • TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256 Run the following command on each node: 210 | P a g e ps -ef | grep kubelet If the --tls-cipher-suites argument is present, ensure it only contains values included in this set. If it is not present check that there is a Kubelet config file specified by --config , and that file sets TLSCipherSuites: to only include values from this set.
column8: If using a Kubelet config file, edit the file to set TLSCipherSuites: to TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256,TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256,TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_CHACHA20_POLY1305,TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384,TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_CHACHA20_POLY1305,TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384 ,TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384,TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256 or to a subset of these values. If using executable arguments, edit the kubelet service file /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubeadm.conf on each worker node and set the --tls-cipher-suites parameter as follows, or to a subset of these values. --tls-cipher-suites=TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256,TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256,TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_CHACHA20_POLY1305,TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384,TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_CHACHA20_POLY1305,TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384,TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384,TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256 Based on your system, restart the kubelet service. For example: systemctl daemon-reload systemctl restart kubelet.service

column4: Ensure that the CNI in use supports Network Policies
column5: There are a variety of CNI plugins available for Kubernetes. If the CNI in use does not support Network Policies it may not be possible to effectively restrict traffic in the cluster.
column6: Kubernetes network policies are enforced by the CNI plugin in use. As such it is important to ensure that the CNI plugin supports both Ingress and Egress network policies.
column7: Review the documentation of CNI plugin in use by the cluster, and confirm that it supports Ingress and Egress network policies.
column8: If the CNI plugin in use does not support network policies, consideration should be given to making use of a different plugin, or finding an alternate mechanism for restricting traffic in the Kubernetes cluster.

column4: Consider external secret storage
column5: Consider the use of an external secrets storage and management system, instead of using Kubernetes Secrets directly, if you have more complex secret management needs. Ensure the solution requires authentication to access secrets, has auditing of access to and use of secrets, and encrypts secrets. Some solutions also make it easier to rotate secrets.
column6: Kubernetes supports secrets as first-class objects, but care needs to be taken to ensure that access to secrets is carefully limited. Using an external secrets provider can ease the management of access to secrets, especially where secrests are used across both Kubernetes and non-Kubernetes environments.
column7: Review your secrets management implementation.
column8: Refer to the secrets management options offered by your cloud provider or a third-party secrets management solution.
column14: By default, no external secret management is configured.
column19: While some aspects of secret management can be automated, such as checking for the presence of Kubernetes Secrets using `kubectl`, the comprehensive evaluation of an external secrets management solution's features—like authentication, auditing, and encryption—requires manual review. This involves assessing documentation and configurations that are not directly accessible or verifiable through Kubernetes APIs or CLI tools.
column21: 1. **Review Current Implementation:** - Use `kubectl get secrets --all-namespaces` to determine if Kubernetes Secrets are being used directly. - Identify if an external secrets management solution is in place. 2. **Verify External Solution Features:** - Ensure the solution requires authentication to access secrets by reviewing its configuration or documentation. - Check if the solution provides auditing capabilities for access and usage of secrets. - Confirm that secrets are encrypted both at rest and in transit by reviewing the solution's security settings. 3. **Document Findings:** - Record whether the current implementation meets the criteria of requiring authentication, auditing, and encryption. - Note any deficiencies or areas for improvement. 4. **Review Remediation Options:** - If the current setup does not meet the criteria, consult the secrets management options offered by your cloud provider or consider third-party solutions. 5. **Confirm Compliance:** - Ensure that any changes made to improve the secrets management implementation are documented and communicated to relevant stakeholders.

column6: etcd is a highly-available key-value store used by Kubernetes deployments for persistent storage of all of its REST API objects. This data directory should be protected from any unauthorized reads or writes. It should not be readable or writable by any group members or the world.
column7: On the etcd server node, get the etcd data directory, passed as an argument --data-dir , from the below command: ps -ef | grep etcd Run the below command (based on the etcd data directory found above). column8: On the etcd server node, get the etcd data directory, passed as an argument --data-dir , from the below command: ps -ef | grep etcd Run the below command (based on the etcd data directory found above). For example, chmod 700 /var/lib/etcd/default.etcd Impact: None 109 | P a g e
column14: By default, etcd data directory has permissions of 700 .
column19: The task involves checking the file system permissions of the etcd data directory, which is not directly manageable or verifiable through Kubernetes APIs or kubectl commands. Both AI opinions recognize the need for SSH access to the etcd server node to perform these checks, as etcd is a component of the Kubernetes control plane and not directly exposed through Kubernetes interfaces. Automation could be considered using external tools like Ansible, but within the scope of Kubernetes capabilities, this remains a manual task.
column21: 1. SSH into the etcd server node where etcd is running. 2. Execute the command: `ps -ef | grep etcd` to identify the etcd process and determine the data directory specified by the `--data-dir` argument. 3. Use the command: `stat -c %a <data_directory>` (replace `<data_directory>` with the actual path obtained in the previous step) to check the directory permissions. 4. Verify that the output shows permissions of `700` or more restrictive (e.g., `700`, `600`). 5. If the permissions are not `700` or more restrictive, change them using: `chmod 700 <data_directory>`.

column4: Ensure that the --peer-client-cert-auth argument is set to true
column5: etcd should be configured for peer authentication.
column6: etcd is a highly-available key value store used by Kubernetes deployments for persistent storage of all of its REST API objects. These objects are sensitive in nature and should be accessible only by authenticated etcd peers in the etcd cluster.
column7: Run the following command on the etcd server node: ps -ef | grep etcd Verify that the --peer-client-cert-auth argument is set to true . Note: This recommendation is applicable only for etcd clusters. If you are using only one etcd server in your environment then this recommendation is not applicable.
column8: Edit the etcd environment file (for example, /etc/etcd/etcd.conf ) on the etcd server node and set the ETCD_PEER_CLIENT_CERT_AUTH parameter to "true" : ETCD_PEER_CLIENT_CERT_AUTH="true" Edit the etcd startup file (for example, /etc/systemd/system/multi- user.target.wants/etcd.service ) and configure the startup parameter for --peer- client-cert-auth and set it to \"${ETCD_PEER_CLIENT_CERT_AUTH}\" : ExecStart=/bin/bash -c "GOMAXPROCS=$(nproc) /usr/bin/etcd --name=\"${ETCD_NAME}\" --data-dir=\"${ETCD_DATA_DIR}\" --listen-client-urls=\"${ETCD_LISTEN_CLIENT_URLS}\" --peer-client-cert-auth=\"${ETCD_PEER_CLIENT_CERT_AUTH}\"" Based on your system, reload the daemon and restart the etcd service. For example, systemctl daemon-reload systemctl restart etcd.service 121 | P a g e Impact: All peers attempting to communicate with the etcd server will require a valid client certificate for authentication.
column14: Note: This recommendation is applicable only for etcd clusters. If you are using only one etcd server in your environment then this recommendation is not applicable. By default, --peer-client-cert-auth argument is set to false .
column19: The compliance control requires verification of the `--peer-client-cert-auth` argument in the etcd configuration, which is managed outside of Kubernetes and typically requires direct access to the server node. This cannot be automated using Kubernetes APIs or kubectl as etcd configurations are not exposed through these interfaces. Both AI opinions agree that manual inspection is necessary, although the second opinion suggests partial automation through scripts, which is not feasible within the Kubernetes API framework.
column21: 1. SSH into the etcd server node. 2. Execute the command: `ps -ef | grep etcd` to list the etcd process details. 3. Verify that the `--peer-client-cert-auth` argument is set to `true` in the command output. 4. If the argument is not set to `true`, edit the etcd environment file (e.g., `/etc/etcd/etcd.conf`) to ensure `ETCD_PEER_CLIENT_CERT_AUTH="true"`. 5. Check the etcd startup file (e.g., `/etc/systemd/system/multi-user.target.wants/etcd.service`) to confirm the `--peer-client-cert-auth` parameter is correctly configured. 6. If changes are made, reload the systemd daemon with `systemctl daemon-reload` and restart the etcd service using `systemctl restart etcd.service`. 7. Confirm that all peers require a valid client certificate for authentication.

column4: Ensure that the --wal-dir argument is set as appropriate
column5: Store etcd logs separately from etcd data.
column6: etcd is a highly-available key value store used by Kubernetes deployments for persistent storage of all of its REST API objects. These objects are sensitive in nature and should not be mixed with log data. Keeping the log data separate from the etcd data also ensures that those two types of data could individually be safeguarded. Also, you could use a centralized and remote log directory for persistent logging. Additionally, this separation also helps to avoid IO competition between logging and other IO operations.
column7: Run the following command on the etcd server node: ps -ef | grep etcd Verify that --wal-dir argument exists, and it is set as appropriate. At the minimum, it should not be set to the same directory as set for --data-dir argument.

column4: Avoid using Kubernetes Secrets
column5: Avoid using Kubernetes secret .
column6: Kubernetes objects of type secret are intended to hold sensitive information, such as passwords, OAuth tokens, and ssh keys. Its current implementation is very basic. It has plenty of risks as highlighted in the reference links including storing secrets as plaintext. Avoid using Kubernetes secrets until you have devised a mechanism to protect them using your own means.
column7: Run the below command and review if there are any secret objects created in the cluster. kubectl get secrets Ensure that these secret objects are the ones you need and are adequately administered as per your requirements.
column8: Use other mechanisms such as vaults to manage your cluster secrets. Impact: You need to use other mechanisms for managing secrets in your cluster.
column14: By default, Kubernetes automatically creates secrets which contain credentials for accessing the API and it automatically modifies your pods to use this type of secret. Please note that those default token secrets are automatically created and deleting them won't be of any use, because Kubernetes will just recreate them.
column19: While the initial detection of Kubernetes secrets can be automated using `kubectl`, determining whether these secrets are "unnecessary" or "improperly managed" requires a contextual understanding of the application and organizational policies. This assessment is subjective and cannot be fully automated, as it involves evaluating the necessity and management quality of the secrets, which are not properties exposed through Kubernetes APIs.
column21: 1. Execute the command `kubectl get secrets --all-namespaces` to list all secrets in the cluster. 2. Review each secret to determine its necessity by checking if it is referenced by any pods or services. 3. Verify the management quality of each secret by ensuring they are not stored in plaintext and are protected according to organizational security policies. 4. Confirm compliance by ensuring that all secrets are necessary, properly encrypted, and managed according to best practices.

column4: Ensure that the --allow-privileged argument is set to false
column5: Do not allow privileged containers.
column6: The privileged container has all the system capabilities, and it also lifts all the limitations enforced by the device cgroup controller. In other words, the container can then do almost everything that the host can do. This flag exists to allow special use-cases, like running Docker within Docker and hence should be avoided for production workloads.
column7: Run the following command on each node: ps -ef | grep kubelet Verify that the --allow-privileged argument is set to false .
column8: Edit the /etc/kubernetes/config file on each node and set the KUBE_ALLOW_PRIV parameter to "--allow-privileged=false" : KUBE_ALLOW_PRIV="--allow-privileged=false" Based on your system, restart the kubelet service. For example: systemctl restart kubelet.service Impact: You will not be able to run any privileged containers. 147 | P a g e Note: A number of components used by Kubernetes clusters currently make use of privileged containers (e.g. Container Network Interface plugins). Care should be taken in ensuring that the use of such plugins is minimized and in particular any use of privileged containers outside of the kube-system namespace should be scrutinized. Where possible, review the rights required by such plugins to determine if a more fine grained permission set can be applied.

column4: Ensure that the --client-ca-file argument is set as appropriate
column5: Enable Kubelet authentication using certificates.
column6: The connections from the apiserver to the kubelet are used for fetching logs for pods, attaching (through kubectl) to running pods, and using the kubelet’s port -forwarding functionality. These connections terminate at the kubelet’s HTTPS endpoint. By default, the apiserver does not verify the kubelet’s serving certificate, which makes the connection subject to man-in-the-middle attacks, and unsafe to run over untrusted and/or public networks. Enabling Kubelet certificate authentication ensures that the apiserver could authenticate the Kubelet before submitting any requests.

column4: Ensure that the --cadvisor-port argument is set to 0
column5: Disable cAdvisor.
column6: cAdvisor provides potentially sensitive data and there's currently no way to block access to it using anything other than iptables. It does not require authentication/authorization to connect to the cAdvisor port. Hence, you should disable the port.
column7: Run the following command on each node: ps -ef | grep kubelet Verify that --cadvisor-port argument exists and is set to 0 .
column8: Edit the /etc/kubernetes/kubelet file on each node and set the KUBELET_ARGS parameter to "--cadvisor-port=0" : KUBELET_ARGS="--cadvisor-port=0" Based on your system, restart the kubelet service. For example: systemctl restart kubelet.service Impact: cAdvisor will not be available directly. You need to work with /metrics endpoint on the API server.
column14: By default, --cadvisor-port argument is set to 4194 .

column19: The task involves verifying and setting file ownership on the host node's filesystem, which is outside the scope of Kubernetes APIs and `kubectl` commands. Kubernetes focuses on managing resources within the cluster and does not provide direct capabilities to manage host-level file permissions. Therefore, this task requires manual verification or the use of external configuration management tools, which are not part of Kubernetes itself.
column21: 1. SSH into each worker node where the Kubernetes configuration file is located. 2. Run the command: `stat -c %U:%G /etc/kubernetes/config` to check the current ownership. 3. Verify that the output shows `root:root` as the owner and group of the config file. 4. If the ownership is not set to `root:root`, run the command: `chown root:root /etc/kubernetes/config` to remediate the issue. 5. Confirm compliance by re-running the `stat -c %U:%G /etc/kubernetes/config` command to ensure the ownership is now set to `root:root`.

column19: The control involves verifying the `--audit-log-maxsize` argument for the API server, which is typically managed as a static Pod in most Kubernetes environments, not as a Deployment. This requires accessing the static Pod manifest files directly on the control plane nodes, which cannot be done through standard Kubernetes API or CLI commands like `kubectl`. Therefore, manual inspection of the configuration files on the master node is necessary.
column21: 1. SSH into the master node of your Kubernetes cluster. 2. Navigate to the directory containing the static Pod manifests, typically `/etc/kubernetes/manifests/`. 3. Open the `kube-apiserver.yaml` file in a text editor. 4. Locate the `--audit-log-maxsize` argument within the container's command section. 5. Verify that the value is set to `100` or an appropriate value as per your organizational policy. 6. If the value is not set correctly, edit the file to set `--audit-log-maxsize=100` and save the changes. 7. Restart the kubelet service to apply the changes.

column4: Ensure that a unique Certificate Authority is used for etcd
column5: Use a different certificate authority for etcd from the one used for Kubernetes.
column6: etcd is a highly available key-value store used by Kubernetes deployments for persistent storage of all of its REST API objects. Its access should be restricted to specifically designated clients and peers only. Authentication to etcd is based on whether the certificate presented was issued by a trusted certificate authority. There is no checking of certificate attributes such as common name or subject alternative name. As such, if any attackers were able to gain access to any certificate issued by the trusted certificate authority, they would be able to gain full access to the etcd database.
column7: Review the CA used by the etcd environment and ensure that it does not match the CA certificate file used for the management of the overall Kubernetes cluster. Run the following command on the master node: ps -ef | grep apiserver Verify that the file referenced by the --client-ca-file for apiserver is different from the --trusted-ca-file used by etcd.

column4: Client certificate authentication should not be used for users
column5: Kubernetes provides the option to use client certificates for user authentication. However as there is no way to revoke these certificates when a user leaves an organization or loses their credential, they are not suitable for this purpose. It is not possible to fully disable client certificate use within a cluster as it is used for component to component authentication.
column6: With any authentication mechanism the ability to revoke credentials if they are compromised or no longer required, is a key control. Kubernetes client certificate authentication does not allow for this due to a lack of support for certificate revocation.
column7: Review user access to the cluster and ensure that users are not making use of Kubernetes client certificate authentication.
column8: Alternative mechanisms provided by Kubernetes such as the use of OIDC should be implemented in place of client certificates.
column10: External mechanisms for authentication generally require additional software to be deployed.
column14: Client certificate authentication is enabled by default.
column15: The lack of certificate revocation was flagged up as a high risk issue in the recent Kubernetes security audit. Without this feature, client certificate authentication is not suitable for end users.
column19: The task of identifying whether client certificate authentication is being used by users cannot be directly automated using Kubernetes APIs or CLI commands. Additionally, the second AI opinion suggests examining the kube-apiserver configuration or audit logs, which are not directly accessible through standard Kubernetes CLI commands for this specific purpose. Therefore, a manual review is necessary to ensure compliance.

column6: The connections from the apiserver to the kubelet are used for fetching logs for pods, attaching (through kubectl) to running pods, and using the kubelet’s port-forwarding functionality. These connections terminate at the kubelet’s HTTPS endpoint. By default, the apiserver does not verify the kubelet’s serving certificate, which makes the connection subject to man-in-the-middle attacks, and unsafe to run over untrusted and/or public networks. Enabling Kubelet certificate authentication ensures that the apiserver could authenticate the Kubelet before submitting any requests.

column5: Allow Kubelet to manage iptables.
column6: Kubelets can automatically manage the required changes to iptables based on how you choose your networking options for the pods. It is recommended to let kubelets manage the changes to iptables. This ensures that the iptables configuration remains in sync with pods networking configuration. Manually configuring iptables with dynamic pod network configuration changes might hamper the communication between pods/containers and to the outside world. You might have iptables rules too restrictive or too open.

column5: Do not override node hostnames.
column6: Overriding hostnames could potentially break TLS setup between the kubelet and the apiserver. Hence, you should setup your kubelet nodes with resolvable FQDNs and avoid overriding the hostnames with IPs.

column4: Ensure that the --tls-cert-file and --tls-private-key-file arguments are set as appropriate
column5: Setup TLS connection on the Kubelets.
column6: The connections from the apiserver to the kubelet are used for fetching logs for pods, attaching (through kubectl) to running pods, and using the kubelet’s port-forwarding functionality. These connections terminate at the kubelet’s HTTPS endpoint. By default, the apiserver does not verify the kubelet’s serving certificate, which makes the connection subject to man-in-the-middle attacks, and unsafe to run over untrusted and/or public networks.
column7: Run the following command on each node: ps -ef | grep kubelet Verify that the --tls-cert-file and --tls-private-key-file arguments exist and they are set as appropriate. If these arguments are not present, check that there is a Kubelet config specified by --config and that it contains appropriate settings for tlsCertFile and tlsPrivateKeyFile.
column8: If using a Kubelet config file, edit the file to set tlsCertFile to the location of the certificate file to use to identify this Kubelet, and tlsPrivateKeyFile to the location of the corresponding private key file. If using command line arguments, edit the kubelet service file /etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf on each worker node and set the below parameters in KUBELET_CERTIFICATE_ARGS variable. --tls-cert-file=<path/to/tls-certificate-file> --tls-private-key-file=<path/to/tls-key-file> Based on your system, restart the kubelet service. For example: systemctl daemon-reload systemctl restart kubelet.service
column19: The kubelet TLS configuration is not directly accessible via standard Kubernetes API or `kubectl` commands, as these configurations are typically managed at the node level and stored in files like `/var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml`. This requires node-level access to verify the presence and correctness of the `tlsCertFile` and `tlsPrivateKeyFile` settings, which cannot be automated through Kubernetes API alone.

column4: Verify that the RotateKubeletServerCertificate argument is set to true
column5: Enable kubelet server certificate rotation.
column6: RotateKubeletServerCertificate causes the kubelet to both request a serving certificate after bootstrapping its client credentials and rotate the certificate as its existing credentials expire. This automated periodic rotation ensures that the there are no downtimes due to expired certificates and thus addressing availability in the CIA security triad. Note: This recommendation only applies if you let kubelets get their certificates from the API server. In case your kubelet certificates come from an outside authority/tool (e.g. Vault) then you need to take care of rotation yourself.

column4: Ensure that the Kubelet only makes use of Strong Cryptographic Ciphers
column5: Ensure that the Kubelet is configured to only use strong cryptographic ciphers.
column6: TLS ciphers have had a number of known vulnerabilities and weaknesses, which can reduce the protection provided by them. By default Kubernetes supports a number of TLS ciphersuites including some that have security concerns, weakening the protection provided.
column7: The set of cryptographic ciphers currently considered secure is the following: • TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256 • TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256 • TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_CHACHA20_POLY1305 • TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384 • TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_CHACHA20_POLY1305 • TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384 • TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384 • TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256 Run the following command on each node: ps -ef | grep kubelet If the --tls-cipher-suites argument is present, ensure it only contains values included in this set. If it is not present check that there is a Kubelet config file specified by --config , and that file sets TLSCipherSuites: to only include values from this set.
column8: If using a Kubelet config file, edit the file to set TLSCipherSuites: to TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256,TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256,TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_CHACHA20_POLY1305,TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384 ,TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_CHACHA20_POLY1305,TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384,TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384,TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256 or to a subset of these values. If using executable arguments, edit the kubelet service file /etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf on each worker node and set the --tls-cipher-suites parameter as follows, or to a subset of these values. --tls-cipher-suites=TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256,TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM _SHA256,TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_CHACHA20_POLY1305,TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM _SHA384,TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_CHACHA20_POLY1305,TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384,TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384,TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256 Based on your system, restart the kubelet service. For example: systemctl daemon-reload systemctl restart kubelet.service
column10: Kubelet clients that cannot support modern cryptographic ciphers will not be able to make connections to the Kubelet API.

column4: Ensure that the CNI in use supports Network Policies
column5: There are a variety of CNI plugins available for Kubernetes. If the CNI in use does not support Network Policies it may not be possible to effectively restrict traffic in the cluster.
column6: Kubernetes network policies are enforced by the CNI plugin in use. As such it is important to ensure that the CNI plugin supports both Ingress and Egress network policies.
column7: Review the documentation of CNI plugin in use by the cluster, and confirm that it supports Ingress and Egress network policies.
column8: If the CNI plugin in use does not support network policies, consideration should be given to making use of a different plugin, or finding an alternate mechanism for restricting traffic in the Kubernetes cluster.
column14: This will depend on the CNI plugin in use.
column15: One example here is Flannel ( https://github.com/coreos/flannel ) which does not support Network policy unless Calico is also in use.
column19: While some aspects of identifying the CNI plugin can be automated using Kubernetes CLI commands, the critical part of this compliance control involves verifying the CNI plugin's documentation to ensure it supports both Ingress and Egress network policies. This verification step is inherently manual as it requires human judgment to interpret and confirm the capabilities documented by the CNI provider. The automation suggested does not fully cover the compliance requirement as it cannot verify documentation.
column21: 1. **Identify the CNI Plugin in Use:** - Run the command `kubectl get nodes -o jsonpath='{.items[*].status.nodeInfo.containerRuntimeVersion}'` to gather information about the container runtime version, which may give clues about the CNI plugin. - Alternatively, check the CNI configuration files directly on the nodes, typically located in `/etc/cni/net.d/`. 2. **Verify CNI Plugin Documentation:** - Access the official documentation of the identified CNI plugin. - Confirm that the documentation explicitly states support for both Ingress and Egress network policies. 3. **Expected Secure Configuration:** - Ensure the documentation confirms that the CNI plugin supports Kubernetes Network Policies, including both Ingress and Egress. 4. **Confirm Compliance:** - Document the findings and ensure that the CNI plugin's capabilities align with the compliance requirements.

column4: Consider external secret storage
column5: Consider the use of an external secrets storage and management system, instead of using Kubernetes Secrets directly, if you have more complex secret management needs. Ensure the solution requires authentication to access secrets, has auditing of access to and use of secrets, and encrypts secrets. Some solutions also make it easier to rotate secrets.
column6: Kubernetes supports secrets as first-class objects, but care needs to be taken to ensure that access to secrets is carefully limited. Using an external secrets provider can ease the management of access to secrets, especially where secrets are used across both Kubernetes and non-Kubernetes environments.
column7: Review your secrets management implementation.
column8: Refer to the secrets management options offered by your cloud provider or a third-party secrets management solution.
column14: By default, no external secret management is configured.
column19: The compliance control requires a comprehensive review of the external secrets management solution, which involves checking authentication mechanisms, auditing capabilities, encryption, and secret rotation features. These aspects are specific to the external tool being used and often require manual inspection of documentation and configurations, which cannot be fully automated using Kubernetes APIs or `kubectl`. While some aspects like the presence of Kubernetes Secrets can be checked using `kubectl`, the detailed verification of external solutions is inherently manual.
column21: 1. Review your current secrets management implementation: - Check if you are using Kubernetes Secrets directly or an external secrets management solution by examining deployment configurations, Helm charts, or CI/CD pipelines. 2. Verify the authentication mechanism: - Ensure the external secrets management solution requires authentication to access secrets by reviewing the tool's documentation (e.g., HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager). 3. Check for auditing capabilities: - Confirm that the solution provides auditing of access to and use of secrets by reviewing audit logs and monitoring configurations. 4. Validate encryption of secrets: - Ensure secrets are encrypted both at rest and in transit by checking the configuration settings of the secrets management tool. 5. Review secret rotation capabilities: - Check if the solution offers features for easier secret rotation by reviewing the documentation or configuration settings.

column4: Ensure that a unique Certificate Authority is used for etcd
column5: Use a different certificate authority for etcd from the one used for Kubernetes.
column6: etcd is a highly available key-value store used by Kubernetes deployments for persistent storage of all of its REST API objects. Its access should be restricted to specifically designated clients and peers only. Authentication to etcd is based on whether the certificate presented was issued by a trusted certificate authority. There is no checking of certificate attributes such as common name or subject alternative name. As such, if any attackers were able to gain access to any certificate issued by the trusted certificate authority, they would be able to gain full access to the etcd database.
column7: Review the CA used by the etcd environment and ensure that it does not match the CA certificate file used for the management of the overall Kubernetes cluster. Run the following command on the master node: ps -ef | grep etcd Note the file referenced by the --trusted-ca-file argument. Run the following command on the master node: ps -ef | grep apiserver Verify that the file referenced by the --client-ca-file for apiserver is different from the --trusted-ca-file used by etcd.
column8: Follow the etcd documentation and create a dedicated certificate authority setup for the etcd service. Then, edit the etcd pod specification file /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml on the master node and set the below parameter. --trusted-ca-file=</path/to/ca-file>
